title: Understand equivalent ratios in the real world
description: This exercise builds an understanding of what it means for ratios to be equivalent or not be equivalent.
topic_path: Get ready for rates & proportional relationships
Understand equivalent ratios in the real world

assessment_items.question_content:
Safety standards require a pool to add $3$ chlorine tablets to every $2{,}000$ liters of water.  Today, the lifeguards at Park Pool added $8$ chlorine tablets to their pool's $10{,}000$ liters of water.  

**How does Park Pool's chlorine compare to the safety standards?**

[[☃ radio 1]]
hints:
###Strategy

To compare ratios, it helps to write equivalent ratios where one of the values is the same.

We want to know whether the *chlorine* is higher or lower than the standard. So let's write equivalent ratios where the amount of *water* is the same.

###Creating equivalent ratios

The standard's ratio of chlorine tablets to liters of water is $\blueE{3}:\maroonD{2{,}000}$.

We can scale up the ratio on a double number line.

[[☃ image 1]]

###Comparing when the water is the same

With $\maroonD{10{,}000}$ liters of water, the standards require $\blueE{15}$ chlorine tablets.

Park Pool's ratio only has $\purpleD{8}$ chlorine tablets for the same amount of water.

Park Pool has *too little* chlorine.
Park Pool has less chlorine than the safety standards require.

choices.content: Park Pool has more chlorine than the safety standards require.
Park Pool has less chlorine than the safety standards require.
Park Pool has the right amount of chlorine.

assessment_items.question_content:
Wendy thinks the perfect chocolate chip cookie has $35$ chocolate chips for every $5$ cookies.  Cookie Club makes cookies with $100$ chocolate chips for every $15$ cookies.

**What will Wendy think about the chocolate in Cookie Club's cookies?**

[[☃ radio 1]]
hints:
###Strategy

To compare ratios, it helps to write equivalent ratios where one of the values is the same.

We want to know whether the amount of *chocolate* is higher or lower than in Wendy's perfect cookies. So let's write equivalent ratios where the number of *cookies* is the same.

###Creating equivalent ratios

The ratio of chocolate chips to cookies in Wendy's perfect cookies is $\blueE{35}:\maroonD{5}$.

We can scale up the ratio on a double number line.

[[☃ image 1]]

###Comparing when the number of cookies is the same

With $\maroonD{15}$ cookies, Wendy's perfect cookies have  $\blueE{105}$ chocolate chips.

The Cookie Club cookies only have $\purpleD{100}$ chocolate chips for the same number of cookies.

The Cookie Club cookies have *too few* chocolate chips.
Wendy will think the Cookie club cookies have too little chocolate.

choices.content: Cookie Club's cookies have too much chocolate.
Cookie Club's cookies have too little chocolate.
Cookie Club's cookies are just right.

assessment_items.question_content:
Ari thinks the perfect milkshake has $5$ scoops of ice cream for every $3$ spoonfuls of caramel.  Freeze Zone makes batches of milkshakes with $10$ scoops of ice cream and $7$ spoonfuls of caramel.

**What will Ari think about the amount of caramel in Freeze Zone's milkshakes?**

[[☃ radio 1]]
hints:
###Strategy

To compare ratios, it helps to write equivalent ratios where one of the values is the same.

We want to know whether the amount of *caramel* is higher or lower than in Ari's perfect milkshake. So let's write equivalent ratios where the amount of *ice cream* is the same.

###Creating equivalent ratios

The ratio of spoonfuls of caramel to scoops of ice cream in Ari's perfect milkshake is $\blueE{3}:\maroonD{5}$.

We can scale up the ratio on a double number line.

[[☃ image 1]]

###Comparing when the amount of ice cream is the same

With $\maroonD{10}$ scoops of ice cream, Ari's perfect milkshakes have $\blueE{6}$ spoonfuls of caramel.

The Freeze Zone's milkshakes have $\purpleD{7}$ spoonfuls of caramel for the same amount of ice cream.

Freeze Zone has **too much** caramel.
Freeze Zone's milkshakes have too much caramel for Ari.

choices.content: Freeze Zone's milkshakes have too much caramel.
Freeze Zone's milkshakes have too little caramel.
Freeze Zone's milkshakes are just right.

assessment_items.question_content:
Burger Barn makes a dipping sauce by mixing $4$ spoonfuls of honey with $1$ spoonful of mustard.  Sandwich Town makes a dipping sauce by mixing $8$ spoonfuls of honey with $2$ spoonfuls of mustard.

**Which dipping sauce has a stronger mustard flavor?**

[[☃ radio 1]]
hints:
###Strategy

To compare ratios, it helps to write equivalent ratios where one of the values is the same.

We want to know whether the amount of *mustard* is higher in Burger Barn's sauce or Sandwich Town's sauce. So let's write equivalent ratios where the amount of *honey* is the same.

###Creating equivalent ratios

The ratio of spoonfuls of honey to spoonfuls of mustard in Burger Barn's sauce is $\blueE{4}:\maroonD{1}$.

We can scale up the ratio on a double number line.

[[☃ image 1]]

###Comparing when the amount of honey is the same

With $\blueE{8}$ spoonfuls of honey, Burger Barn's sauce has $\maroonD{2}$ spoonfuls of mustard.

Sandwich Town's sauce also has $\purpleD{2}$ spoonfuls of mustard for the same amount of honey.

The ratios are equivalent.
The two dipping sauces taste the same.

choices.content: Burger Barn's dipping sauce
Sandwich Town's dipping sauce
The two dipping sauces taste the same.

assessment_items.question_content:
When Arjun makes strawberry milk, he adds $90\,\text{mL}$ of milk for every $20\text{ mL}$ of strawberry syrup.  Arjun's brother, Eli, adds $180\,\text{mL}$ of milk for every $45\text{ mL}$ of strawberry syrup. 

**Which strawberry milk has a stronger strawberry taste?**

[[☃ radio 1]]
hints:
###Strategy

To compare ratios, it helps to write equivalent ratios where one of the values is the same.

We want to know whether the amount of *strawberry syrup* is higher in Arjun's or Eli's strawberry milk. So let's write equivalent ratios where the amount of *milk* is the same.

###Creating equivalent ratios

The ratio of milliliters of strawberry syrup to milliliters of milk in Arjun's strawberry milk is $\blueE{20}:\maroonD{90}$.

We can scale up the ratio on a double number line.

[[☃ image 1]]

###Comparing when the amount of milk is the same

With $\maroonD{180\,\text{mL}}$ of milk, Arjun's strawberry milk has $\blueE{40\,\text{mL}}$ of strawberry syrup.

Eli's strawberry milk has $\purpleD{45\,\text{mL}}$ of strawberry syrup for the same amount of milk.

Eli's strawberry milk has **more** strawberry syrup.
Eli's strawberry milk has a stronger strawberry taste.

choices.content: Arjun's strawberry milk
Eli's strawberry milk
The drinks have an equal strawberry taste.

assessment_items.question_content:
Vic's favorite cookie recipe uses $250$ grams of flour for every $170$ grams of butter. Imani made cookies using $750$ grams of flour for every $630$ grams of butter.

**What will Vic think of the amount of butter in Imani's cookies?** 

[[☃ radio 1]]
hints:
###Strategy

To compare ratios, it helps to write equivalent ratios where one of the values is the same.

We want to know whether the amount of *butter* is higher or lower than in Vic's favorite recipe. So let's write equivalent ratios where the amount of *flour* is the same.

###Creating equivalent ratios

The ratio of grams of butter to grams of flour in Vic's favorite recipe is $\blueE{170}:\maroonD{250}$.

We can scale up the ratio on a double number line.

[[☃ image 1]]

###Comparing when the amount of flour is the same

With $\maroonD{750\,\text{g}}$ of flour, Vic's favorite recipe has $\blueE{510\,\text{g}}$ of butter.

Imani's cookies have $\purpleD{630\,\text{g}}$ of butter for the same amount of flour.

Imani's cookies have *too much* butter.
Vic will think Imani's cookies have too much butter.

choices.content: Imani's cookies have too much butter.
Imani's cookies don't have enough butter.
Imani's cookies have the right amount of butter.

assessment_items.question_content:
Kermit's favorite iced tea uses $15$ tea bags in every $2$ liters of water. Peggy made a $12$-liter batch of iced tea with $90$ tea bags. Peggy and Kermit keep the bags in the water the same amount of time.

**What will Kermit think of Peggy's iced tea?** 

[[☃ radio 1]]
hints:
###Strategy

To compare ratios, it helps to write equivalent ratios where one of the values is the same.

We want to know whether the amount of *tea* is higher or lower than in Kermit's iced tea. So let's write equivalent ratios where the amount of *water* is the same.

###Creating equivalent ratios

The ratio of tea bags to liters of water in Kermit's iced tea is $\blueE{15}:\maroonD{2}$.

We can scale up the ratio on a double number line.

[[☃ image 1]]

###Comparing when the amount of water is the same

With $\maroonD{12\,\text{L}}$ of water, Kermit's iced tea has $\blueE{90}$ tea bags.

Peggy's iced tea also has $\purpleD{90}$ tea bags for the same amount of water.

The ratios are equivalent.
Kermit will think Peggy's iced tea is just right.

choices.content: Peggy's iced tea is too strong.
Peggy's iced tea is too weak.
Peggy's iced tea is just right.

assessment_items.question_content:
A recipe for fruit punch calls for $3$ liters of orange juice and $2$ liters of pineapple juice.  Jo creates a drink by mixing $4$ liters of orange juice with $3$ liters of pineapple juice.  

**Which recipe will have the stronger pineapple taste?**

[[☃ radio 1]]
hints:
###Strategy

To compare ratios, it helps to write equivalent ratios where one of the values is the same.

We want to know whether the *pineapple* taste is stronger in one recipe. So let's write equivalent ratios where the amount of *orange juice* is the same.

###Creating equivalent ratios

The fruit punch drink's ratio of pineapple juice to orange juice is $\blueE{2}:\maroonD{3}$.

We can scale up the recipe on a double number line.

[[☃ image 1]]

Jo's drink's ratio of pineapple juice to orange juice is $\purpleD{3}:\maroonD{4}$.

Let's make a double number line for that ratio, too.

[[☃ image 1]]

###Comparing when the orange juice is the same

We have the value of $\maroonD{12}$ liters of orange juice on both number lines.

[[☃ image 1]]

[[☃ image 2]]

With $\maroonD{12}$ liters of orange juice, the fruit punch recipe uses $\blueE{8}$ liters of pineapple juice, and Jo's drink recipe uses $\purpleD{9}$ liters of pineapple juice.

Jo's drink has more pineapple juice for the same amount of orange juice.

###Conclusion

Jo's drink will have a stronger pineapple taste.

choices.content: Jo's drink will have a stronger pineapple taste.
The fruit punch will have a stronger pineapple taste.
The drinks will taste the same.

assessment_items.question_content:
The Soup Shack usually makes tomato soup with $9$ tomatoes for every $12$ bowls of soup.  Today, they used $6$ tomatoes to make $8$ bowls of soup.

**How does the tomato taste in today's soup compared to the usual recipe?**

[[☃ radio 1]]
hints:
###Strategy

To compare ratios, it helps to write equivalent ratios where one of the values is the same.

We want to know whether the *tomato* taste is stronger or weaker in today's recipe. So let's write equivalent ratios where the amount of *soup* is the same.

###Creating equivalent ratios

The usual ratio of tomatoes to bowls of soup is $\blueE{9}:\maroonD{12}$.

We can scale up the recipe on a double number line.

[[☃ image 1]]

Today's ratio of tomatoes to bowls of soup is $\purpleD{6}:\maroonD{8}$.

Let's make a double number line for that ratio, too.

[[☃ image 1]]

###Comparing when the amount of soup is the same

We have the value of $\maroonD{24}$ bowls of soup on both number lines.

[[☃ image 1]]

[[☃ image 2]]

With $\maroonD{24}$ bowls of soup, the usual recipe uses $\blueE{18}$ tomatoes, and today's recipe also uses $\purpleD{18}$ tomatoes.

The ratios are equivalent.
Today's soup will taste the same.

choices.content: Today's soup has a weaker tomato taste.
Today's soup has a stronger tomato taste.
Today's soup will taste the same.

assessment_items.question_content:
Lashawn makes scrambled eggs with $3$ spoonfuls of salsa for every $2$ eggs.  Gilberta adds $7$ spoonfuls of salsa for every $5$ eggs.

**Whose scrambled eggs have a stronger salsa taste?**

[[☃ radio 1]]
hints:
###Strategy

To compare ratios, it helps to write equivalent ratios where one of the values is the same.

We want to know whether the *salsa* taste is stronger in one recipe. So let's write equivalent ratios where the number of *eggs* is the same.

###Creating equivalent ratios

Lashawn's ratio of spoonfuls of salsa to eggs is $\blueE{3}:\maroonD{2}$.

We can scale up the recipe on a double number line.

[[☃ image 1]]

Gilberta's ratio of spoonfuls of salsa to eggs is $\purpleD{7}:\maroonD{5}$.

Let's make a double number line for that ratio, too.

[[☃ image 1]]

###Comparing when the eggs are the same

We have the value of $\maroonD{10}$ eggs on both number lines.

[[☃ image 1]]

[[☃ image 2]]

With $\maroonD{10}$ eggs, Lashawn uses $\blueE{15}$ spoonfuls of salsa, and Gilberta uses $\purpleD{14}$ spoonfuls of salsa.

Lashawn uses more salsa for the same number of eggs.
Lashawn's scrambled eggs have the stronger salsa taste.

choices.content: Lashawn's eggs
Gilberta's eggs
The dishes of scrambled eggs have equal salsa tastes.

assessment_items.question_content:
Emily thinks the perfect tomato sauce has $15$ cloves of garlic in every $6 \text{ L}$ of sauce. Raphael's tomato sauce has $25$ cloves of garlic in every $9 \text{ L}$ of sauce.

**What will Emily think of the amount of garlic in Raphael's tomato sauce?** 

[[☃ radio 1]]
hints:
###Strategy

To compare ratios, it helps to write equivalent ratios where one of the values is the same.

We want to know whether the *garlic* taste is stronger or weaker in Raphael's recipe. So let's write equivalent ratios where the amount of *sauce* is the same.

###Creating equivalent ratios

Emily's ratio of garlic cloves to liters of sauce is $\blueE{15}:\maroonD{6}$.

We can scale up the recipe on a double number line.

[[☃ image 1]]

Raphael's ratio of garlic cloves to liters of sauce is $\purpleD{25}:\maroonD{9}$.

Let's make a double number line for that ratio, too.

[[☃ image 1]]

###Comparing when the amount of tomato sauce is the same

We have the value of $\maroonD{18\,\text{L}}$ of tomato sauce on both number lines.

[[☃ image 1]]

[[☃ image 2]]

With $\maroonD{18\,\text{L}}$ of tomato sauce, Emily uses $\blueE{45}$ cloves of garlic, and Raphael uses $\purpleD{50}$ cloves of garlic.

Raphael uses more garlic for the same amount of sauce.
Emily will think Raphael's sauce has too much garlic.

choices.content: Raphael's tomato sauce has too much garlic.
Raphael's tomato sauce has too little garlic.
Raphael's tomato sauce is perfect.

assessment_items.question_content:
Nayeli lights a $4\,\text{m}^2$ space with $12$ candles. Citlali lights a $10\,\text{m}^2$ space with $30$ of the same type of candles.

**Whose space is lit brighter?** 

[[☃ radio 1]]
hints:
###Strategy

To compare ratios, it helps to write equivalent ratios where one of the values is the same.

We want to know whether the *candle brightness* is stronger in Nayeli's or Citali's space. So let's write equivalent ratios where the amount of *space* is the same.

###Creating equivalent ratios

Nayeli's ratio of square meters of space to candles is $\blueE{4}:\maroonD{12}$.

We can scale up the ratio on a double number line.

[[☃ image 1]]

Citlali's ratio of square meters of space to candles is $\blueE{10}:\purpleD{30}$.

Let's make a double number line for that ratio, too.

[[☃ image 1]]

###Comparing when the amount of space is the same

We have the value of $\blueE{20\,\text{m}^2}$ of space on both number lines.

[[☃ image 1]]

[[☃ image 2]]

With $\blueE{20\,\text{m}^2}$ of space, Nayeli lights $\maroonD{60}$ candles, and Citlali also lights $\purpleD{60}$ candles.

The ratios are equivalent.
The spaces are equally bright.

choices.content: Nayeli's area
Citlali's area
The spaces are equally bright.
